---
title: "Commute"
description: |
  Getting from home to work, unfortunately
date: '2026-03-06'
image: "commute.jpg"
categories:
    - DC
    - Adventure
format: html
toc: true
lightbox: true
execute:
  echo: false
  warning: false
  error: false
  message: false
---

```{r}
library(here)
source(here("packages.R"))
source(here("functions.R"))

series_filter = 9

data = pollresults(series_filter)
```

```{r}
simple_bar_question(data, "Mode")
```

```{r}
simple_bar_question(data, "Audio")
```

```{r}
simple_bar_question(data, "Route")
```

```{r}
simple_bar_question(data, "Biking")
```

```{r}
simple_bar_question(data, "Escalators")
```

# How decisive were these results?

```{r}
#| label: summary-stats
summary_commute = summarize_two_option(data)
```

**TL;DR:** Escalators produced the most lopsided result of the series so far — Escalator Walkers stomped Escalator Standers `r summary_commute %>% filter(question == "Escalators") %>% pull(leader_votes)`–`r summary_commute %>% filter(question == "Escalators") %>% pull(trailer_votes)` (`r summary_commute %>% filter(question == "Escalators") %>% pull(leader_share) %>% percent(1)`), with a p-value so small it barely fits on the table below. Audio, on the other hand, was basically a coin flip: Podcasts & Audiobooks edged Music by a single vote, `r summary_commute %>% filter(question == "Audio") %>% pull(leader_votes)`–`r summary_commute %>% filter(question == "Audio") %>% pull(trailer_votes)`.

```{r}
#| label: summary-table
binom_summary_table(summary_commute)
```

```{r}
#| label: fig-leader-ci
#| fig-cap: "Leader shares with 95% confidence intervals."
#| fig-width: 7
#| fig-height: 4
leader_ci_plot(summary_commute)
```

```{r}
#| label: fig-waffle-escalators
#| fig-cap: "One square per vote: Escalators."
#| fig-width: 6
#| fig-height: 4
waffle_question(data, "Escalators")
```

# Are escalator walkers also the multi-modal commuters?

Both "walk the escalator" and "use more than one mode" sound like the choices of someone optimizing their commute down to the minute. Let's see how much those two groups could overlap.

```{r}
#| label: overlap-escalator-mode
ov_walk_multi = overlap_bounds(
  a_total = data %>% filter(question == "Escalators", option == "Escalator Walker") %>% pull(votes),
  N_a     = data %>% filter(question == "Escalators") %>% pull(N) %>% unique(),
  b_total = data %>% filter(question == "Mode", option == "Multi-modal") %>% pull(votes),
  N_b     = data %>% filter(question == "Mode") %>% pull(N) %>% unique()
)
```

Of the `r ov_walk_multi$m` overlapping voters, somewhere between `r ov_walk_multi$min_overlap` and `r ov_walk_multi$max_overlap` (`r percent(ov_walk_multi$min_overlap/ov_walk_multi$m, 1)`–`r percent(ov_walk_multi$max_overlap/ov_walk_multi$m, 1)`) could be both Escalator Walkers *and* Multi-modal commuters, versus about `r round(ov_walk_multi$expected_overlap)` (`r percent(ov_walk_multi$expected_overlap/ov_walk_multi$m, 1)`) expected under independence. Given how dominant Escalator Walker already is on its own, the "efficient commuter" coalition is large no matter how you slice it — we just can't say exactly how large without matched responses.
